Mixture Score-Based Diffusion Models
with Multiple Choice Learning
Ayouba Deba
PSL Research University вҖ” M2 IASD
April 20, 2026
Abstract
Score-based diffusion models have emerged as a powerful class of generative models, yet a
single denoising network must implicitly represent all modes of the data distribution, which
can limit both sample quality and diversity. This report explores the combination of score-
based diffusion with Multiple Choice Learning (MCL), a structured prediction framework
that trains K competing expert networks with a stochastic Winner-Takes-All (WTA) loss.
The methodology is applied to MNIST with K = 4 experts, with four collapse-mitigation
training variants (hard, annealed, relaxed, and resilient WTA), and a study of five inference-
time routing strategies: a single expert over the full trajectory, per-step random and best-
norm heuristics, score mixture averaging, and a learned gating network. A dedicated cross-
variant ablation on an A100-80GB GPU is conducted and shows that the training objective
fundamentally shapes inference-time behaviour: hard and resilient WTA produce a single
dominant expert (yielding a best FID of 13.49 with gating at K = 4, but an effective
ensemble size of one; pushing K to 8 under resilient MCL further lowers this to the global
best of 13.00 via hidden-expert routing), while annealed and relaxed WTA produce genuinely
multi-skilled experts (gating reaches FID = 88.25, strictly beating the monolithic baseline at
111.44, with Precision 0.644 and Recall 0.836). A rigorous distinction between inter-class and
intra-class diversity is provided, along with a formalisation of the resulting quality-versus-
true-mixture trade-off; the methodologically-correct reading of MCL+diffusion is argued to
be the annealed-WTA regime, in which the gating network provides a non-trivial multi-expert
improvement over both the baseline and any single expert.

1 Introduction
Diffusion models [1, 2] learn to reverse a gradual noising process, transforming simple Gaussian
noise into samples from a complex data distribution. In the continuous-time variance-exploding
(VE) formulation, one trains a neural network ОөОё(xt, Пғ) to predict the noise component of a
corrupted sample, and new data is generated by solving a deterministic probability-flow ordinary
differential equation (ODE).
While a single model can in principle capture the entire data distribution, in practice its
finite capacity forces compromises: modes may be under-represented, and the model may average
between distinct structures instead of committing to one. Multiple Choice Learning (MCL) [3, 4]
addresses this by training K parallel expert networks under a Winner-Takes-All (WTA) objective
that encourages specialization. Each expert becomes responsible for a distinct region of the
output space, forming a Voronoi-like partition.
The central question of this project is: how should one route between specialized
experts at inference time, and how does the training objective shape the set of
meaningful routing strategies? Five strategies of increasing sophistication are implemented
and compared (single expert, per-step random, per-step best-norm, score mixture, learned gat-
ing) and their effects are analyzed on the qualityвҖ“diversity trade-off. A second question, which
emerges empirically from the experiments in this report, is whether the WTA training ob-
jective produces a genuine mixture of experts at all: the experiments show that this
depends sharply on the collapse-mitigation strategy used during training, and that the standard
reading of вҖңMCL + learned gating beats the baselineвҖқ holds cleanly only in the regime where
all experts acquire non-trivial competence.
Related Works. This report sits at the intersection of three lines of work вҖ” score-based
generative modelling, Multiple Choice Learning, and mixture-of-experts inference вҖ” summarised
below.
вҖўScore-based diffusion models. Ho et al. [1] introduced the DDPM framework, training
a neural network to denoise samples corrupted by a fixed Gaussian Markov chain. Song
et al. [2] unified DDPM with score-matching into a continuous-time SDE formulation,
and introduced the two parameterisations used here: the variance-exploding (VE) and
variance-preserving (VP) SDEs, together with the deterministic probability-flow ODE that
enables fewer-step sampling. Karras et al. [5] (EDM) studied the design space of noise-
preconditioning, weighting, and sampler choices; the VE formulation adopted here follows
with log-uniform Пғ-sampling during training and a geometric Пғ-grid at inference, together
with the Heun second-order ODE integrator they recommend.
вҖўMultiple Choice Learning. The WTA loss was introduced by GuzmГЎn-Rivera, Ba-
tra, and Kohli [3] for structured prediction tasks where multiple plausible outputs exist
for a single input. Lee et al. [4] extended this to diverse deep ensembles with stochastic
gradient descent, and documented the hypothesis-collapse failure mode that motivates all
four training variants studied here. Perera, Letzelter et al. [8] recently proposed Annealed
MCL, replacing the hard arg min with a temperature-scheduled softmax (Eq. (12)); Let-
zelter et al. [9] concurrently proposed Resilient MCL, which introduces per-expert learned
scoring heads trained by cross-entropy against the true error-argmin (Eq. (15)). We are
not aware of prior work combining MCL with score-based diffusion in this exact setup and
systematically comparing the four training variants on a common benchmark.
вҖўMixture-of-experts inference and gating. Per-step expert selection in an ODE inte-
grator is conceptually close to mixture-of-experts architectures [10], but this setting differs
in three key ways: (i) experts are not conditioned on a learned gating during training вҖ”
routing is implicit in the WTA loss; (ii) routing at inference time is applied sequentially
3

2.4 Training Details
The baseline model is trained for 200 epochs on MNIST (60,000 training images normalized to
[вҲ’1, 1]) with the following configuration, which is shared across the baseline and every MCL
variant:
вҖўOptimizer: Adam with learning rate О· = 3 Г—10вҲ’4, default (ОІ1, ОІ2) = (0.9, 0.999), no
learning-rate schedule (constant О· for 200 epochs вҖ” empirical observation showed that
cosine annealing did not improve final FID on this architecture/dataset).
вҖўBatch size = 512, which, combined with 60,000 training images, gives вҢҠ60000/512вҢӢвүҲ117
optimizer steps per epoch and вүҲ23,400 steps over the 200-epoch run.
вҖўMixed-precision training with loss scaling; gradients are unscaled before clipping.
вҖўGradient clipping by global norm at 1.0.
вҖўExponential moving average of the network weights with decay ОІ = 0.999, used for
every sampling and evaluation call.
вҖўПғ is sampled log-uniformly in [Пғmin, Пғmax] = [0.01, 80] for training, and on a geometric grid
of N = 200 values for inference ODE integration.
вҖўSingle fixed random seed = 42.
вҖўHardware: one NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB, вүҲ30 minutes of wall-clock time per variant.
All generated samples are clamped to [вҲ’1, 1] before metric computation and visualisation.
3 Multiple Choice Learning & Routing Strategies
3.1 MCL with Stochastic Winner-Takes-All
Multiple Choice Learning replaces the single model ОөОё with K independent expert networks
{ОөОёk }Kk=1, each with its own parameters, optimizer, and EMA. In the experiments reported here,
K = 4.
For a given training tuple (x0, Оө, Пғ), each expert produces a prediction ЛҶОөk = ОөОёk (xt, Пғ). The
winner for sample n is the expert with the smallest per-sample reconstruction error:

3.2 Expert Collapse and Mitigation Strategies
A well-documented failure mode of the hard WTA objective is hypothesis collapse (also called
expert collapse) [4]: early in training, one expert may gain a slight advantage on a large fraction
of samples, causing it to receive more gradient updates, which in turn makes it win even more
often. This positive feedback loop starves the remaining experts of training signal, and they
converge to degenerate solutions. In the experiments with K = 4 and pure hard WTA, this
is precisely what is observed: Expert 0 monopolizes nearly all assignments, while Experts 1вҖ“3
collapse to near-constant outputs (see Figure 12).
Several mitigation strategies have been proposed in the literature and are described below:
Annealed WTA [8]. Rather than applying hard winner selection from the start, Annealed
MCL begins by updating all experts on every sample (equivalent to independent training with
a uniform assignment) and progressively anneals toward hard WTA over the course of training.
Concretely, the hard indicator 1[k = kвӢҶn] of Eq. (11) is replaced by a soft assignment weight
given by a negative-loss softmax with temperature П„ :

weight (ensemble training); at П„ вҶ’ 0 the softmax collapses to the hard argmin and Eq. (12)
recovers Eq. (11). This gives every expert a chance to develop meaningful representations
before competition begins, preventing early monopolization. The full training loop is given in
Algorithm 2.
Relaxed WTA. A simpler alternative retains the hard winner assignment but distributes a
small fraction of the gradient to losing experts. For a winner set Sk = {n : kвӢҶn = k} and its
complement  М„Sk = {n : kвӢҶn Мё= k}, expert kвҖҷs loss is
Lrlxk = 1
|Sk|+ Оұ | М„Sk|

where Оұ вҲҲ (0, 1) is a fixed hyperparameter. The winner therefore receives full gradient weight
(1) while each loser receives weight Оұ вүӘ1 (Оұ = 0.1 in the experiments here). This ensures that
non-winning experts continue to improve on the broader data distribution and are less likely to
fall irreversibly behind. Algorithm 3 summarises the procedure.
Resilient MCL [9]. Resilient MCL augments each expert with a learned scoring head sПҶk (xt, Пғ) вҲҲ
Rthat predicts the expertвҖҷs own competence on a given input at a given noise level. At train-
ing time, routing is performed by the scoring heads rather than by reconstruction error: the
winner is kвӢҶn = arg maxk sПҶk (x(n)
t , Пғ). The scoring heads are trained jointly with the experts

so the scoring heads learn a discriminator between competent and incompetent experts. Each
ScoringHead is a lightweight вүҲ 10k-parameter network (two Conv2d layers 1 вҶ’ 16 вҶ’ 32 with
stride 2, adaptive average pool to 1 Г—1, concatenation with a 64-dim sinusoidal Пғ-embedding,
MLP head with hidden size 64 producing a scalar competence score). Algorithm 4 gives the full
loop.
The results presented in Sections 4вҖ“5 are obtained with the annealed variant. The annealing
schedule activates three out of four experts (usage fractions вүҲ40%/31%/28%/0%), a substantial
improvement over pure hard WTA where only one expert survived. Expert 3 still collapsed
despite the annealing, possibly due to initialization or optimisation dynamics. The learned
gating network (Section 3.3.3) adapts to this by distributing ODE steps among the three active
experts based on the current noisy state and noise level.
3.3 Inference Routing Strategies
At inference time, one must decide which expert(s) to query at each step of the ODE integration.
Five strategies are implemented and compared, of which three correspond to the core exam
requirements.
3.3.1 Strategy 1: Single Expert for the Full Trajectory
The simplest approach fixes a single expert k and uses ОөОёk for all ODE steps:
xi+1 = xi + (Пғi+1 вҲ’Пғi) ОөОёk (xi, Пғi). (16)
This produces samples from a single вҖңmode clusterвҖқ of the distribution. By enumerating k вҲҲ
{1, . . . , K}, one can inspect the specialization of each expert, but any single run inherently limits
diversity.

TrГЁs bon extrait вҖ” il contient le cЕ“ur de ton mГ©moire.  
Voici une explication claire de ce que dit cette partie (notamment la zone autour de la page/section вҖң8вҖқ).

## IdГ©e principale

Tu combines :

- **Diffusion score-based** (gГ©nГ©ration dвҖҷimages en dГ©bruitant progressivement),
- avec **MCL (Multiple Choice Learning)** : plusieurs experts \(K\) au lieu dвҖҷun seul modГЁle.

Objectif : Г©viter quвҖҷun seul rГ©seau вҖңmoyenne toutвҖқ et perde des modes, en forГ§ant une **spГ©cialisation** des experts.

---

## Ce que montrent tes rГ©sultats (message clГ©)

Le point le plus important de lвҖҷabstract :

- La **mГ©thode dвҖҷentraГ®nement WTA** choisie change complГЁtement le comportement Г  lвҖҷinfГ©rence.
- Certains variants (hard/resilient) donnent souvent un **expert dominant** (efficace en FID mais peu вҖңvrai mГ©langeвҖқ).
- DвҖҷautres (annealed/relaxed) donnent des experts rГ©ellement complГ©mentaires, et le **gating** devient utile.

Donc : вҖңMCL + gatingвҖқ nвҖҷest vraiment pertinent que si lвҖҷentraГ®nement produit une vraie diversitГ© dвҖҷexperts.

---

## Section 3.1 вҖ” WTA stochastique (Algorithme 1)

Pour chaque sample du batch :

1. Chaque expert prГ©dit le bruit.
2. On calcule lвҖҷerreur de chaque expert.
3. On choisit le gagnant \(k^\*\) = expert avec erreur minimale.
4. **Seul le gagnant reГ§oit le gradient** pour ce sample.

Effet recherchГ© : spГ©cialisation (type partition de VoronoГҜ).  
Risque : **collapse** (un expert gagne presque tout, les autres nвҖҷapprennent plus).

---

## Section 3.2 вҖ” Anti-collapse (Algorithmes 2, 3, 4)

### 1) Annealed WTA
Au dГ©but, tous les experts apprennent (assignation soft), puis on durcit progressivement vers hard WTA via une tempГ©rature \(\tau\).

- \(\tau\) grande : partage quasi uniforme.
- \(\tau\) petite : presque winner-takes-all.

Avantage : limite le monopole prГ©coce dвҖҷun expert.

### 2) Relaxed WTA
On garde un gagnant, mais les perdants reГ§oivent quand mГӘme un petit poids \(\alpha\) dans la loss.

Avantage : les perdants continuent dвҖҷapprendre, donc moins de collapse.

### 3) Resilient MCL
On ajoute des **scoring heads** qui apprennent Г  prГ©dire quel expert est compГ©tent.

- Le routage se fait via ces scores.
- Les scores sont entraГ®nГ©s pour mimer вҖңqui aurait eu la plus petite erreur rГ©elleвҖқ.

Avantage : routage appris explicitement ; mais peut quand mГӘme converger vers un expert dominant selon la dynamique.

---

## Section 3.3 вҖ” Routage Г  lвҖҷinfГ©rence

Tu dois dГ©cider quel expert utiliser pendant lвҖҷintГ©gration ODE.

Exemple montrГ© :  
**Strategy 1 = Single expert full trajectory**  
вҶ’ on fixe un expert pour toute la trajectoire.  
Simple, mais diversitГ© limitГ©e.

Les autres stratГ©gies (random per-step, best-norm, mixture, gating) testent des routages plus dynamiques.

---

## Lecture вҖңmГ©thodologiquement correcteвҖқ de tes rГ©sultats

Ton argument est fort et cohГ©rent :

- Si un variant produit un expert unique dominant, le gain peut venir dвҖҷun вҖңmeilleur expertвҖқ, pas dвҖҷun vrai mГ©lange.
- Le cas le plus convaincant pour вҖңmixture of expertsвҖқ est le rГ©gime **annealed WTA** (ou relaxed), oГ№ plusieurs experts sont rГ©ellement actifs et le gating apporte un gain non trivial.

---

1 Introduction
Diffusion models [1, 2] learn to reverse a gradual noising process, transforming simple Gaussian
noise into samples from a complex data distribution. In the continuous-time variance-exploding
(VE) formulation, one trains a neural network ОөОё(xt, Пғ) to predict the noise component of a
corrupted sample, and new data is generated by solving a deterministic probability-flow ordinary
differential equation (ODE).
While a single model can in principle capture the entire data distribution, in practice its
finite capacity forces compromises: modes may be under-represented, and the model may average
between distinct structures instead of committing to one. Multiple Choice Learning (MCL) [3, 4]
addresses this by training K parallel expert networks under a Winner-Takes-All (WTA) objective
that encourages specialization. Each expert becomes responsible for a distinct region of the
output space, forming a Voronoi-like partition.
The central question of this project is: how should one route between specialized
experts at inference time, and how does the training objective shape the set of
meaningful routing strategies? Five strategies of increasing sophistication are implemented
and compared (single expert, per-step random, per-step best-norm, score mixture, learned gat-
ing) and their effects are analyzed on the qualityвҖ“diversity trade-off. A second question, which
emerges empirically from the experiments in this report, is whether the WTA training ob-
jective produces a genuine mixture of experts at all: the experiments show that this
depends sharply on the collapse-mitigation strategy used during training, and that the standard
reading of вҖңMCL + learned gating beats the baselineвҖқ holds cleanly only in the regime where
all experts acquire non-trivial competence.
Related Works. This report sits at the intersection of three lines of work вҖ” score-based
generative modelling, Multiple Choice Learning, and mixture-of-experts inference вҖ” summarised
below.
вҖўScore-based diffusion models. Ho et al. [1] introduced the DDPM framework, training
a neural network to denoise samples corrupted by a fixed Gaussian Markov chain. Song
et al. [2] unified DDPM with score-matching into a continuous-time SDE formulation,
and introduced the two parameterisations used here: the variance-exploding (VE) and
variance-preserving (VP) SDEs, together with the deterministic probability-flow ODE that
enables fewer-step sampling. Karras et al. [5] (EDM) studied the design space of noise-
preconditioning, weighting, and sampler choices; the VE formulation adopted here follows
with log-uniform Пғ-sampling during training and a geometric Пғ-grid at inference, together
with the Heun second-order ODE integrator they recommend.
вҖўMultiple Choice Learning. The WTA loss was introduced by GuzmГЎn-Rivera, Ba-
tra, and Kohli [3] for structured prediction tasks where multiple plausible outputs exist
for a single input. Lee et al. [4] extended this to diverse deep ensembles with stochastic
gradient descent, and documented the hypothesis-collapse failure mode that motivates all
four training variants studied here. Perera, Letzelter et al. [8] recently proposed Annealed
MCL, replacing the hard arg min with a temperature-scheduled softmax (Eq. (12)); Let-
zelter et al. [9] concurrently proposed Resilient MCL, which introduces per-expert learned
scoring heads trained by cross-entropy against the true error-argmin (Eq. (15)). We are
not aware of prior work combining MCL with score-based diffusion in this exact setup and
systematically comparing the four training variants on a common benchmark.
вҖўMixture-of-experts inference and gating. Per-step expert selection in an ODE inte-
grator is conceptually close to mixture-of-experts architectures [10], but this setting differs
in three key ways: (i) experts are not conditioned on a learned gating during training вҖ”
routing is implicit in the WTA loss; (ii) routing at inference time is applied sequentially
3
along an ODE trajectory, so coherence across steps is a separate concern from per-step
competence; (iii) the gating network trained in Section 3.3.3 is a post-hoc classifier over
winner labels produced by the trained experts, rather than a jointly-trained gating com-
ponent. The Pareto analysis in Section 4.3 shows that these three distinctions are not
academic: strategies that would be standard in MoE (uniform or score-weighted mix-
ing) are catastrophic in the diffusion-MCL setting when WTA training has produced true
mode-exclusive specialists.
вҖўEvaluation metrics. Evaluation relies on FrГ©chet Inception Distance (FID) [6] and
the Improved Precision/Recall metric of KynkГӨГӨnniemi et al. [7]. Because the standard
Inception-v3 features are poorly calibrated for 28 Г— 28 grayscale digits, a small CNN
classifier is used instead, trained on MNIST itself (Section 4.1); this choice is discussed in
the caveat paragraph at the end of Section 4.2.
Summary.
вҖўCross-variant ablation of MCL training objectives for score-based diffusion. We
implemented the full training and inference pipeline for four collapse-mitigation variants
(hard, annealed, relaxed, and resilient WTA) and combined them with five inference rout-
ing strategies (single expert, per-step random, per-step best-norm, score mixture, and a
learned gating network), yielding a systematic cross-tabulation of training objective against
inference rule under FID, Precision, and Recall.
вҖўTwo qualitatively distinct regimes of MCL+diffusion. We isolated a strong-specialist
regime (hard, resilient), in which any per-step routing collapsed to catastrophic FID and
the learned gating reduced to expert-indicator inference, from a soft-specialist regime (an-
nealed, relaxed), in which all experts acquired overlapping competence and gating provided
a genuinely multi-expert improvement over the monolithic baseline.
вҖўK and batch size as implicit anti-collapse regularisers. We ran two orthogo-
nal sweeps over K вҲҲ {2, 3, 4, 6, 8} and B вҲҲ {128, 256, 512, 1024} and observed a phase-
transition-like jump between K = 2 and K = 3 under annealed WTA, a non-monotone
gated-FID optimum at K = 6, and a sharp shift from B = 256 to B = 1024 where the
gated FID improved from 131 to 25.74 (all in this single-seed protocol).
вҖўHidden-expert routing at resilient K = 8. We showed that, at K = 8 under resilient
MCL, the default expert was dead (single-expert FID = 3206) yet the learned gating
network located a surviving expert among the eight and routed every ODE step to it,
yielding the global best FID of the study (13.00) вҖ” an effectively single-expert operating
point that establishes the upper bound on the quality axis.
вҖўQuality-versus-true-mixture trade-off. We proposed a rigorous distinction between
inter-class and intra-class diversity, from which we argued that the annealed-WTA con-
figuration is the methodologically-correct instantiation of MCL+diffusion, while the hard
and resilient results were reframed as upper-bound benchmarks obtained through expert
collapse rather than through a principled mixture mechanism.

2.2 Network Architecture
The noise predictor ОөОё is parameterized as a U-Net with residual blocks. The noise level Пғ is
encoded via a sinusoidal positional embedding applied to log Пғ/4, followed by a two-layer MLP
producing a time embedding vector of dimension dt = 128. The U-Net consists of an encoderвҖ“
bottleneckвҖ“decoder structure with skip connections. Each residual block applies GroupNorm,
3Г—3 convolutions, and additive time conditioning. Downsampling and upsampling are performed
via strided and nearest-neighbor convolutions, respectively. With a base channel width of 32
and channel multipliers (1, 2), the architecture is compact yet expressive enough for the 28 Г—28
grayscale MNIST images.
An exponential moving average (EMA) of the model weights with decay ОІ = 0.999 is main-
tained throughout training and used for all inference.
2.3 Deterministic Sampling via Probability Flow ODE
Sampling proceeds by solving the probability-flow ODE that transports the prior N(0, Пғ2maxI)
to the data distribution. In the VE parameterization, the ODE takes the form

integrated from Пғ = Пғmax down to Пғ вүҲ0. The noise schedule for integration is constructed as a
geometric progression:

Euler discretization. The simplest integrator applies a first-order update at each step:
xi+1 = xi + (Пғi+1 вҲ’Пғi) ОөОё(xi, Пғi). (8)
Heun discretization. A second-order Heun (improved Euler) method first computes a pre-
dictor step using (8) to obtain ЛҶxi+1, then refines:
xi+1 = xi + Пғi+1 вҲ’Пғi
2

provided Пғi+1 > 0; at the final step, a plain Euler update is used. The Heun method requires
two network evaluations per step but yields substantially more accurate trajectories.
2.4 Training Details
The baseline model is trained for 200 epochs on MNIST (60,000 training images normalized to
[вҲ’1, 1]) with the following configuration, which is shared across the baseline and every MCL
variant:
вҖўOptimizer: Adam with learning rate О· = 3 Г—10вҲ’4, default (ОІ1, ОІ2) = (0.9, 0.999), no
learning-rate schedule (constant О· for 200 epochs вҖ” empirical observation showed that
cosine annealing did not improve final FID on this architecture/dataset).
вҖўBatch size = 512, which, combined with 60,000 training images, gives вҢҠ60000/512вҢӢвүҲ117
optimizer steps per epoch and вүҲ23,400 steps over the 200-epoch run.
вҖўMixed-precision training with loss scaling; gradients are unscaled before clipping.
вҖўGradient clipping by global norm at 1.0.
вҖўExponential moving average of the network weights with decay ОІ = 0.999, used for
every sampling and evaluation call.
вҖўПғ is sampled log-uniformly in [Пғmin, Пғmax] = [0.01, 80] for training, and on a geometric grid
of N = 200 values for inference ODE integration.
вҖўSingle fixed random seed = 42.
вҖўHardware: one NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB, вүҲ30 minutes of wall-clock time per variant.
All generated samples are clamped to [вҲ’1, 1] before metric computation and visualisation.

3.2 Expert Collapse and Mitigation Strategies
A well-documented failure mode of the hard WTA objective is hypothesis collapse (also called
expert collapse) [4]: early in training, one expert may gain a slight advantage on a large fraction
of samples, causing it to receive more gradient updates, which in turn makes it win even more
often. This positive feedback loop starves the remaining experts of training signal, and they
converge to degenerate solutions. In the experiments with K = 4 and pure hard WTA, this
is precisely what is observed: Expert 0 monopolizes nearly all assignments, while Experts 1вҖ“3
collapse to near-constant outputs (see Figure 12).
Several mitigation strategies have been proposed in the literature and are described below:
Annealed WTA [8]. Rather than applying hard winner selection from the start, Annealed
MCL begins by updating all experts on every sample (equivalent to independent training with
a uniform assignment) and progressively anneals toward hard WTA over the course of training.
Concretely, the hard indicator 1[k = kвӢҶn] of Eq. (11) is replaced by a soft assignment weight
given by a negative-loss softmax with temperature П„ :

The results presented in Sections 4вҖ“5 are obtained with the annealed variant. The annealing
schedule activates three out of four experts (usage fractions вүҲ40%/31%/28%/0%), a substantial
improvement over pure hard WTA where only one expert survived. Expert 3 still collapsed
despite the annealing, possibly due to initialization or optimisation dynamics. The learned
gating network (Section 3.3.3) adapts to this by distributing ODE steps among the three active
experts based on the current noisy state and noise level.
3.3 Inference Routing Strategies
At inference time, one must decide which expert(s) to query at each step of the ODE integration.
Five strategies are implemented and compared, of which three correspond to the core exam
requirements.
3.3.1 Strategy 1: Single Expert for the Full Trajectory
The simplest approach fixes a single expert k and uses ОөОёk for all ODE steps:
xi+1 = xi + (Пғi+1 вҲ’Пғi) ОөОёk (xi, Пғi). (16)
This produces samples from a single вҖңmode clusterвҖқ of the distribution. By enumerating k вҲҲ
{1, . . . , K}, one can inspect the specialization of each expert, but any single run inherently limits
diversity.
9
3.3.2 Strategy 2: Per-Step Expert Selection
One can select a different expert at each integration step according to a heuristic rule. Two
variants are considered:
Random selection. At each step i, an expert ki is drawn uniformly at random. This intro-
duces stochasticity into the otherwise deterministic ODE, often producing incoherent samples
because successive steps may follow inconsistent denoising directions.
Best-expert selection. At each step, all K experts are evaluated and the one with the
smallest output norm вҲҘЛҶОөkвҲҘ2 is selected:
kвӢҶi = arg mink
вҲҘвҲҘОөОёk (xi, Пғi)вҲҘвҲҘ2. (17)
While intuitively appealing, this heuristic lacks a principled justification and can lead to degen-
erate outputs.
3.3.3 Strategy 3: Learned Gating Network
The most principled approach trains an auxiliary gating network gПҶ(xt, Пғ) вҲҲ RK that predicts
which expert would win the WTA competition for a given noisy input and noise level. Training
data is collected by running the MCL experts on the training set and recording the winner
indices (10). The gating network is then trained with cross-entropy loss:
Lgate(ПҶ) = вҲ’Ext,Пғ

ki = arg maxk

The gating network is a lightweight CNN with two convolutional layers (channels 1 вҶ’32 вҶ’
64), average pooling, and a time-conditioned MLP head producing K logits. It is trained for 25
epochs on winner labels collected from 50 batches of the training data.

4.1 Experimental Setup
All models are trained on MNIST (28 Г—28 grayscale images, normalized to [вҲ’1, 1]) with K = 4
MCL experts, using the full optimisation configuration described in Section 2.4 (Adam, constant
LR 3 Г—10вҲ’4, no scheduler, batch 512, AMP, EMA 0.999, grad clip 1.0, 200 epochs, seed 42).

4.4 Ablation on the number of experts K
The cross-variant study of Section 4.3 holds K = 4 fixed. A natural follow-up question is whether
the regime itself (soft- vs. strong-specialist) is a property of the training variant, or whether it also
depends on the number of experts. The full pipeline was therefore re-run for K вҲҲ {2, 3, 4, 6, 8}
on both the annealed and the resilient variants, keeping every other hyperparameter identical to
the main setup (B = 512, 200 epochs, seed = 42). Tables 3 and 4 report the complete FIDs; the
full per-expert sample grids are collected in Appendix B. Three non-trivial phenomena emerge.
Phase transition between K = 2 and K = 3 in annealed WTA. Table 3 shows that
at K = 2, even annealed WTA produces a strong-specialist system indistinguishable from
hard/resilient WTA: single-expert FID = 13.90, gated FID = 13.91, and every per-step mix-
ing strategy collapses to FID > 600. In other words, with only two experts the annealing
schedule is insufficient to maintain both alive: the later phase of training (low П„ ) converges to
a near-hard WTA and one of the two experts monopolises. Starting at K = 3, the regime flips:
single-expert FID jumps to 530 (no expert owns the whole data on its own), mixture-score drops
to 211 (partial compatibility between experts), random per-step to 316, and best-norm to a re-
markably low 32.05 вҖ” below the K = 4 value of 287.86 вҖ” because with only three experts the
вҖңleast-confidentвҖқ heuristic no longer systematically picks a collapsed one. The learned gating,
correspondingly, finds a genuinely multi-expert routing: FID = 103.13, slightly better than the
K = 4 value of 88.25 once the gap between single-expert and gated is compared (530вҲ’103 = 427
at K = 3; 339 вҲ’88 = 251 at K = 4). This suggests that the soft-specialist regime claimed in
Section 4.3 is not simply a property of the annealed objective, but of annealed-at-sufficient-K.
K = 6 is the optimum of the annealed K-sweep at B = 512. The most striking result
of the K-sweep is that the annealed gated FID is non-monotone in K and peaks at K = 6.

Dynamical Regimes of Diffusion Models
Giulio Biroli

Abstract
Using statistical physics methods, we study generative diffusion models in the regime where the dimension of space and the number of data are large, and the score function has been trained optimally. Our analysis reveals three distinct dynamical regimes during the backward generative diffusion process. The generative dynamics, starting from pure noise, encounters first a вҖҷspeciationвҖҷ transition where the gross structure of data is unraveled, through a mechanism similar to symmetry breaking in phase transitions. It is followed at later time by a вҖҷcollapseвҖҷ transition where the trajectories of the dynamics become attracted to one of the memorized data points, through a mechanism which is similar to the condensation in a glass phase. For any dataset, the speciation time can be found from a spectral analysis of the correlation matrix, and the collapse time can be found from the estimation of an вҖҷexcess entropyвҖҷ in the data. The dependence of the collapse time on the dimension and number of data provides a thorough characterization of the curse of dimensionality for diffusion models. Analytical solutions for simple models like high-dimensional Gaussian mixtures substantiate these findings and provide a theoretical framework, while extensions to more complex scenarios and numerical validations with real datasets confirm the theoretical predictions.

Keywords: Diffusion Models 
|
 Generative diffusion 
|
 Phase Transitions 
|
 Memorization 
|
 Collapse

Introduction
Machine learning has recently witnessed thrilling advancements, especially in the realm of generative models. At the forefront of this progress are diffusion models (DMs), which have emerged as powerful tools for modeling complex data distributions and generating new realistic samples. They have become the state of the art in generating images, videos, audio or 3d scenes [43, 45, 46, 22, 48, 42, 3, 37]. Although the practical success of generative diffusion models is widely recognized, their full theoretical understanding remains an open challenge. Rigorous results assessing their convergence on finite dimensional data have been obtained in [18, 30, 17, 5, 15, 13]. However, realistic data live in high dimensional spaces, where interpolation between datapoints should face the curse of dimensionality [20]. A thorough understanding of how generative models escape this curse is still lacking. This requires approaches able to take into account that the number and the dimension of the data are simultaneously very large. In this work we face this challenge using statistical physics methods which have been developed to study probability distributions, disordered systems and stochastic processes in high-dimensions [33, 12, 8].

Diffusion models work in two stages. In the forward diffusion one starts from a data point (e.g., an image) and gradually adds noise to it, until the image has become pure noise. In the backward process one gradually denoises the image using a diffusion in an effective force field (the вҖңscoreвҖқ) which is learnt leveraging techniques from score matching [26, 47] and deep neural networks. In this study, we focus on diffusion models which are efficient enough to learn the exact empirical score, i.e. the one obtained by noising the empirical distribution of data. In practical implementations, this should happen when one performs a long training of a strongly over-parameterized deep network to learn the score, in the situation when the number of data is not too large.

Within this framework we develop a theoretical approach able to characterize the dynamics of diffusion models in the simultaneous limit of large dimensions and large dataset. We show that the backward generative diffusion process consists of three subsequent dynamical regimes. The first one is basically pure Brownian motion. In the second one the backward trajectory finds one of the main classes of the data (for instance if the data consists of images of horses and cars, a given trajectory will specialize towards one of these two categories). In the third regime, the diffusion вҖҳcollapsesвҖҷ onto one of the examples of the dataset: a given trajectory commits to the attraction basin of one of the data points, and the backward evolution brings it back to that exact data point. Since diffusion models are defined as the time reversal of a forward noising process, the generative process has to collapse on the training dataset under the exact empirical score assumption [9, 23]. We show, by performing a thorough analysis of the curse of dimensionality for diffusion models, that this memorization can be avoided at finite times only if the size of the dataset is exponentially large in the dimension. An alternative, which is the one used in practice, is to rely on regularization and approximate learning of the score, departing from its exact form. Understanding this crucial aspect of generative diffusion is a key open challenge [27, 49, 16] for which analyzing what happens when the exact empirical score is used represents a first step.

Separating these three dynamical regimes, we identify two characteristic cross-over times. The speciation time 
t
S
 is the transition from pure noise to the commitment of a trajectory towards one category. The collapse time 
t
C
 is the time where the backward trajectory falls into the attractor of one given data point. We provide mathematical tools to predict these times in terms of structure of data. We will first study simple models such as high-dimensional Gaussian mixtures, where we obtain a full analytical solution and hence a very detailed understanding. Within this setting, we show that in the simultaneous limit of large number and dimension of the data, the speciation and collapse cross-overs become sharp thresholds. Interestingly, both of them are related to phase transitions studied in physics. We then extend our results to more general settings and discuss the key role played by the dimensionality of data and the number of samples. Finally, we perform numerical experiments, and confront the theory to real data such as CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and LSUN, showing that our main findings hold in realistic cases. We conclude highlighting the consequences and the guidelines offered by our results, and discussing the next research steps, in particular how to go beyond the вҖңexact empirical scoreвҖқ framework.

Our main contribution is the characterization of three dynamical regimes in the time-reversed process in the limit of large number of data and large dimension. In regime I, at the beginning of the backward process, the random dynamical trajectories generated by (3) have not committed to a particular class of data. They have roughly the same probability to end up in one of the two classes. Figure 1 illustrates this behavior by showing that trajectories corresponding to different final classes are within a single bundle (or tube). In regime II, instead, the dynamical trajectories have committed to a particular class. In this case, the trajectory will remain in the same class until the end of the backward process. During regime II, the backward dynamics generates the features needed to produce samples in a given class, but the fate of the trajectory in terms of class is sealed. In analogy with evolutionary dynamics, we call вҖңspeciationвҖқ the cross-over between regimes I and II, which has also important connections with the concept of symmetry breaking in physics [7, 39]. As we shall show, the speciation cross-over takes place on a time-scale

. In practice, this unwanted phenomenon is avoided by using an approximate score function which is smoother than the exact one, together with a large enough dataset. We will come back to this point later in the discussion.

Our characterization of the backward dynamics is obtained using statistical physics methods developed to study phase transitions, disordered systems, and out-of-equilibrium dynamics in physics. We provide a brief introduction in the SI Appendix. The connection is not only methodological; in fact, our analysis unveils interesting relationships between the transition described above and phenomena intensively studied in physics in the last decades. In particular, the collapse transition is mutatis mutandis a glass transition in which the low energy glass states correspond to the training data. The results we obtain in Eqs. (4,5), which are at the level of rigor of theoretical physics, provide guidelines and testable predictions for realistic applications. Numerical experiments on several subsets of realistic datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and LSUN) confirm their validity.

Generalization to realistic datasets
In the case of Gaussian mixtures we could thoroughly characterize the backward dynamics for large 
n
 and 
d
, using the knowledge of the initial distribution 
P
0
. We now present a more general approach to the speciation and collapse transitions, for cases in which 
P
0

P
0
 is available. In particular, we present arguments to establish criteria from Eqs. (4,5) which can be directly applied to realistic datasets.

The speciation transition can be analyzed using the covariance matrix, 
C
0
, of the initial data. Our assumption that data can be organized in two distinct and very different classes translates, in terms of 
C
0
, in the existence of a strong principal component with eigenvalue 
Оӣ
. The speciation transition can be generically understood in terms of the forward process: it corresponds to the time-scale at which the noise added to the data blurs the principal component, and hence the connection to a given class. On this timescale, the trajectories coming from different classes in the forward process coalesce within the same bundle, as illustrated in Fig. 1. By time-reversal symmetry, it is therefore also on this time that the trajectories in the backward process separate and commit to the different classes. The arguments above offer another way, besides the exact computation by the Random Energy Model method, to illustrate the relationship between the collapse and the glass transition studied in physics [6]. In fact, the ways in which 
P
t
e
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’

x
вҶ’
 before and after the collapse is the exact counterpart of how the Boltzmann law covers the configuration space before and after the ideal glass transition [6]. In the glass phase the Boltzmann law is formed by lumps centered around amorphous optimal configurations (ideal glasses). Whereas in the liquid phase the Boltzmann law is spread over all configurations. In the case studied here, the elements of the training set are the counterparts of the ideal glass configurations.

Having shown the generality of our criteria for speciation and collapse, we now test them on realistic data.

Numerical experiments
In the following we focus on various image datasets and we learn the score function using a state-of-the-art neural network with a finite number of samples 
n
. We assume that, by using a heavily over-parameterized model, the resulting estimate 
в„ұ
^
 of the score is close to the exact one.

Settings and datasets. We train a diffusion model as first described in [25], and corresponding to (1), with a time horizon 
T
=
5.05
 and a linear schedule for the variance. The denoiser is a U-Net [40] with an architecture similar to other traditional implementations of DMs [25, 44, 39]. Our training sets are constructed by focusing on data divided in two classes and extracted from the image datasets: MNIST [29], CIFAR-10 [28], downsampled ImageNet [14], and LSUN [50]. For example, in the case of ImageNet, the two classes we use are panda and seashore (as illustrated in Fig. 1). The large variety of datasets allows us to explore different kinds of images and several values of 
n
 and 
d
, see Table 1. We refer to the SI Appendix for details on the processing of these datasets, the denoiser, and how the numerical experiments presented below are carried out.

In conclusion, the numerical experiments presented in this section demonstrate the presence of speciation and collapse in realistic image datasets, and validate our theory, in particular the criteria from Eqs. (4) and (5) to identify the times at which these phenomena takes place.

Discussion
In this work we have analyzed the backward dynamics of diffusion models assumed to be efficient enough to learn the exact empirical score. We have shown that for large number of data 
n
, large dimension 
d
, and in absence of regularization, the backward dynamics displays three different regimes. We have characterized the cross-overs between them, dubbed speciation and collapse, which become true transitions in the large 
n
,
d
 limit. Interestingly, both of them have physical counterparts in the theory of phase transitions. Speciation is a symmetry-breaking transition of 
P
t
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’
)
 at which the most prominent classes are generated [39, 7]. Collapse corresponds to a glass transition at which 
P
t
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’
)
 fragments in an ensemble of lumps centered around the training data. Our approach characterizes the time at which speciation and collapse take place in terms of structure of data. The speciation time is determined by the eigenvalue of the principal component of the covariance matrix of the data, whereas the collapse time is governed by the entropy of the noised data distribution.
Although we have focused on a specific diffusion model, our results hold for the large variety of DMs which are based on inverting the noising process [1]. These methods use different procedures to implement the denoising process 
P
вҲһ
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’
)
вҶ’
P
t
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’
)
вҶ’
P
0
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’
)
. However, as we have shown, the phenomena of speciation and collapse, can be understood and analyzed focusing on the properties of the distribution 
P
t
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’
)
 alone. In consequence, since all these different DMs lead to the same backward evolution of 
P
t
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’
)
, the criteria from Eqs. (4) and (5) for speciation and collapse hold in general, whether the generative process is deterministic and flow-based or stochastic and diffusion-based.

Our work was done within the вҖңexact empirical scoreвҖқ hypothesis. However, the phenomena we analyzed are relevant also when the score is learned approximately, as shown by our numerical experiments. Moving beyond the вҖңexact empirical scoreвҖқ hypothesis opens up multiple avenues for further research. On the one hand, it would be interesting to analyze simple models of data and score in the limit of large number of data, dimension and parameters, as initiated in [7, 16]. On the other hand, it would be important to develop a quantitative study of the role of regularization on the phenomena we presented in this work. As discussed in [7], using a good model of the score is a key ingredient to generate data in the right proportions, i.e. reproducing the correct weights of the classes. In consequence, regularization methods could be detrimental in regime I and for speciation: by preventing the score to be close to the exact one they could lead to data with correct features (e.g., realistic images) but wrong proportions with respect to the training set. On the contrary, regularization is beneficial to avoid the collapse. As we have shown, diffusion models are вҖңcursedвҖқ: one needs an exponential number of data in 
d
 to avoid the collapse on datapoints from the training set. Regularization allows to circumvent this problem in practice if the number of data is large enough [27] (see also SI Appendix). The volume argument we develop in this work can be applied beyond the вҖңexact empirical scoreвҖқ hypothesis, and could offer a way to analyze quantitatively how the collapse depends on 
n
,
d
 and the capacity of the model used to learn the score.

Finally, our results also provide suggestions to improve and understand procedures used in practical applications. In particular, taking into account in practical implementations the existence of three regimes of the backward dynamics could lead to better performances.

Supplementary Information (SI) Appendix

Dynamical Regimes of Diffusion Models

Giulio Biroli, Tony Bonnaire, Valentin de Bortoli, and Marc MГ©zard

In this Appendix we first present more details on the analytical frameworks we used to study the backward dynamics of DMs, and then provide additional information on the numerical experiments. As already stressed in the main text, we need specific methods to study the limit of large dimension and large number of data. Such methods have been developed in statistical physics to study dynamics and thermodynamics of a large number of degrees of freedom. We will refer to original articles and, when needed, provide a short-introduction to the methods.

Appendix ALandau-type expansion for estimating the speciation time

We conclude this section on regime I and II by stressing that we expect that our analysis can be generalized to other models. The Curie-Weiss already studied in [7] is an example. The dynamics in regime II has also been studied in [21] using a model for the score.

Datasets and preprocessing.
Our experiments are based on five datasets: MNIST [29], CIFAR [28], ImageNet16/32 [14], and LSUN [50]. For each dataset, we focus only on two well-disctinct and balanced classes that are: zero and seven for MNIST (
n
=
10вҖү000
), horses and cars for CIFAR (
n
=
3000
), lesser pandas and seashores for ImageNet16 and 32 (
n
=
2000
), and churches and conference rooms for LSUN (
n
=
40вҖү000
 or 
n
=
200
). In the case of MNIST, the images are zero-padded from their original size to 
32
Г—
32
. For LSUN, images are center-cropped at size 
256
Г—
256
 before being resized to 
64
Г—
64
. All the datasets but MNIST have three color channels that are kept for the training, and the data are centered in each channel. The different datasets therefore allow to span several values of 
d
 ranging from 
1024
 (for MNIST) to 
12вҖү288
 for LSUN. The values of 
Оӣ
 given in the main text corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of the covariance matrix are computed from the first channel only.

Architecture and training.
For all the datasets, the denoiser 
Оҫ
вҶ’
Оё
вҒў
(
x
вҶ’
вҒў
(
t
вҖІ
)
,
t
вҖІ
)
 has a U-Net architecture similar to previous applications of DDPMs [25, 44]. More precisely, it implements four resolution levels, each having two convolutional residual blocks with group normalization, and self attention is applied to the two intermediary levels. The time is added into each block through a standard sinusoidal position embedding, resulting in a total of 
25.7
 million parameters. The models are then trained using ADAM optimizer with a fixed learning rate of 
10
вҲ’
4
 for 
350
k steps with batches of size 
128
, except for LSUN for which we reduced it to 
64
. All the 
n
 images were used to train the models, without any data augmentation. It is worth mentioning that the results exposed in the main text do not depend on the formal architecture of the denoiser, nor the optimization scheme, as long as the network is able to learn a score close to the true one (or equivalently, a sufficiently good denoiser). In Figs. 9 to 10, we show several samples generated from our six trained models.

Speciation experiment.
To estimate numerically the speciation time from the probability 
П•
вҒў
(
t
)
 that the two clones end up in the same class, we need to classify the generated samples. To do so, we train the PyTorch [36] implementation of the ResNet-18 architecture [24] with pre-trained weights on ImageNet. One model is trained for each of the dataset using all the 
n
 samples. The resulting classifiers yield 
95
% test accuracy at worst on LSUN, and more than 
99
% for the other datasets.

Implicit Dynamical Regularization in Training
Tony BonnaireвҖ 
LPENS

tony.bonnaire@phys.ens.fr

Abstract
Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success across a wide range of generative tasks. A key challenge is understanding the mechanisms that prevent their
memorization of training data and allow generalization. In this work, we investigate the role of the training dynamics in the transition from generalization to
memorization. Through extensive experiments and theoretical analysis, we identify
two distinct timescales: an early time П„gen at which models begin to generate
high-quality samples, and a later time П„mem beyond which memorization emerges.
Crucially, we find that П„mem increases linearly with the training set size n, while
П„gen remains constant. This creates a growing window of training times with n
where models generalize effectively, despite showing strong memorization if training continues beyond it. It is only when n becomes larger than a model-dependent
threshold that overfitting disappears at infinite training times. These findings reveal
a form of implicit dynamical regularization in the training dynamics, which allow
to avoid memorization even in highly overparameterized settings. Our results are
supported by numerical experiments with standard U-Net architectures on realistic and synthetic datasets, and by a theoretical analysis using a tractable random
features model studied in the high-dimensional limit. В§
1 Introduction
Diffusion Models [DMs, 49, 20, 54, 55] achieve state-of-the-art performance in a wide variety of
AI tasks such as the generation of images [45], audios [64], videos [33], and scientific data [31, 40].
This class of generative models, inspired by out-of-equilibrium thermodynamics [49], corresponds
to a two-stage process: the first one, called forward, gradually adds noise to a data, whereas the
second one, called backward, generates new data by denoising Gaussian white noise samples. In
DMs, the reverse process typically involves solving a stochastic differential equation (SDE) with a
force field called score. However, it is also possible to define a deterministic transport through an
ordinary differential equation (ODE), treating the score as a velocity field, an approach that is for
instance followed in flow matching [32].
Understanding the generalization properties of score-based generative methods is a central issue
in machine learning, and a particularly important question is how memorization of the training set

Architectural Regularization for n > nвӢҶ

[15, 25]), and Dynamical Regularization, corresponding to a large intermediate generalization regime
obtained when the training dynamics is stopped early, i.e. П„ вҲҲ [П„gen, П„mem].
is avoided in practice. A model without regularization achieving zero training loss only learns the
empirical score, and is bound to reproduce samples of the training dataset at the end of the backward
process. This memorization regime [30, 6] is empirically observed when the training set is small
and disappears when it increases beyond a model-dependent threshold [24]. Understanding the
mechanisms controlling this change of regimes from memorization to generalization is a central
challenge for both theory and applications. Model regularization and inductive biases imposed by the
network architecture were shown to play a role [25, 47], as well as a dynamical regularization due
to the finiteness of the learning rate [62]. However, the regime shift described above is consistently
observed even in models where all these regularization mechanisms are present. This suggests that
the core mechanism behind the transition from memorization to generalization lies elsewhere. In this
work, we demonstrate вҖ“ first through numerical experiments, and then via the theoretical analysis of
a simplified model вҖ“ that this transition is driven by an implicit dynamical bias towards generalizing
solutions emerging in the training, which allows to avoid the memorization phase.
Contributions and theoretical picture. We investigate the dynamics of score learning using
gradient descent, both numerically and analytically, and study the generation properties of the score
depending on the time П„ at which the training is stopped. The theoretical picture built from our
results and combining several findings from the recent literature is illustrated in Fig. 1. The two main
parameters are the size of the training set n and the expressivity of the class of score functions on
which one trains the model, characterized by a number of parameters p; when both n and p are large
one can identify three main regimes. Given p, if n is larger than n
вҲ—
(p) (which depends on the training
set and on the class of scores), the score model is not expressive enough to represent the empirical
score associated to n data, and instead provides a smooth interpolation, approximately independent
of the training set. In this regime, even with a very large training time П„ вҶ’ вҲһ, memorization does
not occur because the model is regularized by its architecture and the finite number of parameters.
When n < nвҲ—
(p) the model is expressive enough to memorize, and two timescales emerge during
training: one, П„gen, is the minimum training time required to achieve high-quality data generation; the
second, П„mem > П„gen, signals when further training induces memorization, and causes the model to
2
increasingly reproduce the training samples (left panel). The first timescale, П„gen, is found independent
of n, whereas the second, П„mem, grows approximately linearly with n, thus opening a large window
of training times during which the model generalizes if early stopped when П„ вҲҲ [П„gen, П„mem]. Our
results shows that implicit dynamical regularization in training plays a crucial role in score-based
generative models, substantially enlarging the generalization regime (see right panel of Fig. 1), and
hence allowing to avoid memorization even in highly overparameterized settings. We find that the key
mechanism behind the widening gap between П„gen and П„mem is the irregularity of the empirical score
at low noise level and large n. In this regime the models used to approximate the score provide a
smooth interpolation that remains stable for a long period of training times and closely approximates
the population score, a behavior likely rooted in the spectral bias of neural networks [42]. Only at
very long training times do the dynamics converge to the low lying minimum corresponding to the
empirical score, leading to memorization (as illustrated in the one-dimensional examples in the left
panel of Fig. 1).
The theoretical picture described above is based on our numerical and analytical results, and builds up
on previous works, in particular numerical analysis characterizing the memorizationвҖ“generalization
transition [18, 63], analytical works on memorization of DMs [15, 25, 24], and studies on the spectral
bias of deep neural networks [42]. Our numerical experiments use a class of scores based on a
realistic U-Net [46] trained on downscaled images of the CelebA dataset [34]. By varying n and p,
we measure the evolution of the sample quality (through FID) and the fraction of memorization during
learning, which support the theoretical scenario presented in Fig. 1. Additional experimental results
on synthetic data are provided in Supplemental Material (SM, Sects. A and B). On the analytical
side, we focus on a class of scores constructed from random features and simplified models of data,
following [15]. In this setting, the timescales of training dynamics correspond directly to the inverse
eigenvalues of the random feature correlation matrix. Leveraging tools from random matrix theory,
we compute the spectrum in the limit of large datasets, high-dimensional data, and overparameterized
models. This analysis reveals, in a fully tractable way, how the theoretical picture of Fig. 1 emerges
within the random feature framework.
Related works.
вҖў The memorization transition in DMs has been the subject of several recent empirical investigations [9, 50, 51] which have demonstrated that state-of-the-art image DMs вҖ“ including Stable
Diffusion and DALLВ·E вҖ“ can reproduce a non-negligible portion of their training data, indicating a form of memorization. Several additional works [18, 63] examined how this phenomenon
is influenced by factors such as data distribution, model configuration, and training procedure,
and provide a strong basis for the numerical part of our work.
вҖў A series of theoretical studies in the high-dimensional regime have analyzed the memorizationвҖ“
generalization transition during the generative dynamics under the empirical score assumption
[6, 1, 57], showing how trajectories are attracted to the training samples. Within this highdimensional framework, [11, 12, 60, 15] study the score learning for various model classes.
In particular, [15] uses a Random Feature Neural Network [43]. The authors compute the
asymptotic training and test losses for П„ вҶ’ вҲһ and relate it to memorization. The theoretical
part of our work generalizes this approach to study the role of training dynamics and early
stopping in the memorizationвҖ“generalization transition.
вҖў Recent works have also uncovered complementary sources of implicit regularization explaining
how DMs avoid memorization. Architectural biases and limited network capacity were for
instance shown to constrain memorization in [25, 24], and finiteness of the learning rate
prevents the model from learning the empirical score in [62]. Also related to our analysis, [29]
provides general bounds showing the beneficial role of early stopping the training dynamics to
enhance generalization for finitely supported target distributions, as well as a study of its effect
for one-dimensional gaussian mixtures.
вҖў Finally, previous studies on supervised learning [42, 65], and more recently on DMs [59], have
shown that deep neural networks display a frequency-dependent learning speed, and hence a
learning bias towards low frequency functions. This fact plays an important role in the results
we present since the empirical score contains a low frequency part that is close to the population
score, and a high-frequency part that is dataset-dependent. To the best of our knowledge, the
training time to learn the high-frequency part and hence memorize, that we find to scale with n,
has not been studied from this perspective in the context of score-based generative methods.

This solution is known to inevitably recreate samples of the training set at the end of the generative
process (i.e., it perfectly memorizes), unless n grows exponentially with the dimension d [6]. However,
this is not the case in many practical applications where memorization is only observed for relatively
small values of n, and disappears well before n becomes exponentially large in d. The empirical
minimization performed in practice, within a given class of models and a given minimization
procedure, does not drive the optimization to the global minimum of (4), but instead to a smoother
estimate of the score that is independent of the training set with good generalization properties [24],
as the global minimum of (3) would do. Understanding how it is possible, and in particular the role
played by the training dynamics to avoid memorization, is the central aim of the present work.
2 Generalization and memorization during training of diffusion models
Data & architecture. We conduct our experiments on the CelebA face dataset [34], which we
convert to grayscale downsampled images of size d = 32 Г— 32, and vary the training set size n from
128 up to 32768. Our score model has a U-Net architecture [46] with three resolution levels and a
base channel width of W with multipliers 1, 2 and 3 respectively. All our networks are DDPMs [20]
trained to predict the injected noise at diffusion time t using SGD with momentum at fixed batch size
min(n, 512). The models are all conditioned on t, i.e. a single model approximates the score at all
times, and make use of a standard sinusoidal position embedding [56] that is added to the features of
each resolution. More details about the numerical setup can be found in SM (Sect. A).
Evaluation metrics. To study the transition from generalization to memorization during training,
we monitor the loss (4) during training using a fixed diffusion time t = 0.01. At various numbers
of SGD updates П„ , we compute the loss on all n training examples (training loss) and on a held-out
test set of 2048 images (test loss). To characterize the score obtained after a training time П„ , we
assess the originality and quality of samples by generating 10K samples using a DDIM accelerated
sampling [52]. We compute (i) the FrГ©chet-Inception Distance [FID, 19] against 10K test samples
which we use to identify the generalization time П„gen; and (ii) the fraction of memorized generated

Role of training set size on the learning dynamics. At fixed model capacity (p = 4 Г— 106
, base
width W = 32), we investigate how the training set size n impacts the previous metrics. In the left
panel of Fig. 2, we first report the FID (solid lines) and fmem(П„ ) (dashed lines) for various n. All
trainings dynamics exhibit two phases. First, the FID quickly decreases to reach a minimum value on
a timescale П„gen (вүҲ 100K) that does not depend on n. In the right panel, the generated samples at
П„ = 100K clearly differ from their nearest neighbors in the training set, indicating that the model
generalizes correctly. Beyond this time, the FID remains flat. fmem(П„ ) is zero until a later time П„mem
after which it increases, clearly signaling the entrance into a memorization regime, as illustrated
by the generated samples in the right-most panel of Fig. 2, very close to their nearest neighbors.
Both the transition time П„mem and the value of the final fraction fmem(П„max) (with П„max being one to
four million SGD steps) vary with n. The inset plot shows the normalized memorization fraction
fmem(П„ )/fmem(П„max) against the rescaled time П„ /n, making all curves collapse and increase at
around П„ /n вүҲ 300, showing that П„mem вҲқ n, and demonstrating the existence of a generalization
window for П„ вҲҲ [П„gen, П„mem] that widens linearly with n, as illustrated in the left panel of Fig. 1.
As highlighted in the introduction, memorization in DMs is ultimately driven by the overfitting of
the empirical score smem(x, t). The evolution of Ltrain(П„ ) and Ltest(П„ ) at fixed t = 0.01 are shown
in the middle panel of Fig. 2 for n ranging from 512 to 32768. Initially, the two losses remain
nearly indistinguishable, indicating that the learned score sОё(x, t) does not depend on the training
set. Beyond a critical time, Ltrain continues to decrease while Ltest increases, leading to a nonzero
generalization loss whose magnitude depends on n. As n increases, this critical time also increases
and, eventually, the training and test loss gap shrinks: for n = 32768, the test loss remains close
to the training loss, even after 11 million SGD steps. The inset shows the evolution of both losses
with П„ /n, demonstrating that the overfitting time scales linearly with the training set size n, just like
П„mem identified in the left panel. Moreover, there is a consistent lag between the overfitting time and
П„mem at fixed n, reflecting the additional training required for the model to overfit the empirical score
sufficiently to reproduce the training samples, and therefore to impact the memorization fraction.

Memorization is not due to data repetition. We must stress that this delayed memorization with
n is not due to the mere repetition of training samples, as a first intuition could suggest. In SM
Sects. A and B, we show that full-batch updates still yield П„mem вҲқ n. In other words, even if at fixed
П„ all models have processed each sample equally often, larger n consistently postpone memorization.
This confirms that memorization in DMs is driven by a fundamental n-dependent change in the loss
landscape вҖ“ not by a sample repetition during training.
Effect of the model capacity. To study more precisely the role of the model capacity on the
memorizationвҖ“generalization transition, we vary the number of parameters p by changing the U-Nets
base width W вҲҲ {8, 16, 32, 48, 64}, resulting in a total of p вҲҲ {0.26, 1, 4, 9, 16} Г— 106 parameters.
In the left panel of Fig. 3, we plot both the FID (top row) and the normalized memorization fraction
(bottom row) as functions of П„ for several width W and training set sizes n. Panels A and C
demonstrate that higher-capacity networks (larger W) achieve high-quality generation and begin to
memorize earlier than smaller ones. Panels B and D show that the two characteristic timescales
simply scale as П„gen вҲқ WвҲ’1
and П„mem вҲқ nWвҲ’1
. In particular, this implies that, for W > 8, the
critical training set size ngm(p) at which П„mem = П„gen is approximately independent of p (at least on
the limited values of p we focused on). When n > ngm(p), the interval [П„gen, П„mem] opens up, so that
early stopping within this window yields high quality samples without memorization. In the right
panel of Fig. 3, we display this boundary (solid line) in the (n, p) plane by fixing the training time to
П„ = П„gen, that we identify numerically using the collapse of all FIDs at around W П„gen вүҲ 3Г—106
(see
panel B), and computing the smallest n such that fmem(П„ ) = 0. The resulting solid curve delineates

generalize perfectly under early stopping. We repeat this experiment for П„ = 3П„gen and П„ = 8П„gen,
showing saturation to larger and larger p as П„ increases. Eventually, for П„ вҶ’ вҲһ, we expect these
successive boundaries to converge to the architectural regularization threshold n

Emergence of the two timescales during training. We first show in Fig. 5 that the behavior of
training and test losses in the RF model mirrors the one found in realistic cases in Sect. 2, with a
separation of timescales П„gen and П„mem which increases with n. Equation (10) is linear in A and
hence it can be solved exactly (see SM). The timescales of the training dynamics are given by the
inverse eigenvalues of the p Г— p matrix вҲҶtU/ПҲp. Building on the Gaussian Equivalence Principle
[GEP, 16, 17, 36] and the theory of linear pencils [7], George et al. (2025) derive a coupled system
of equations characterizing the Stieltjes transform of the eigenvalue density ПҒ(О») of U for isotropic
Gaussian data that lie in a D-dimensional subspace with D вүӨ d and D = O(d). We offer an
alternative derivation presented in SM for general variance using the replica method [37] вҖ“ a heuristic
method from the statistical physics of disordered systems вҖ“ yielding the more compact formulation
for obtaining the spectrum stated in Theorem 3.1. Before stating the theorem, we introduce

The proofs of both theorems are shown in SM (Sect. C). We recall that training timescales are directly
related to eigenvalues О» via the relation П„
вҲ’1 = ПҲp/вҲҶtО»min. Theorem 3.2 therefore demonstrates
the emergence of the two training timescales П„mem and П„gen in the overparametrized regime of the
RFNN model. They are respectively associated to the measures ПҒ1 and ПҒ2, which are well separated
in regime I, for ПҲp вү« ПҲn вү« 1, as shown in Fig. 4.
Generalization: The timescale П„gen on which the first relaxation takes place is associated to the
formation of the generalization regime. It is related to the bulk ПҒ2 and is or order 1/вҲҶt. This regime
only depends on the population covariance ОЈ of the data and is independent of the specific realization
9
of the dataset. On this timescale, which is of order one, both the training Ltrain and test Ltest losses
decrease. The generalization loss Lgen = Ltest вҲ’ Ltrain is zero, and Escore tends to a value that we
find to scale as O(ПҲ
вҲ’О·
n
) with О· вүғ 0.59 numerically (see Fig. 5).
Memorization: The timescale П„mem, on which the second stage of the dynamics takes place, is
associated to overfitting and memorization. It is related to the bulk ПҒ1, and scales as ПҲp/вҲҶtО»min,
where О»min is the left edge of ПҒ1. In the overparameterized regime p вү« n, П„mem becomes large and of
order ПҲn/вҲҶt, thus implying a scaling of П„mem with n. On this timescale, the training loss decreases
while the test loss increases, converging to their respective asymptotic values as computed in [15].
Fig. 5 indeed shows that all training and test curves separate, correspondingly the generalization loss
Lgen increases, at a time that scales with ПҲp/вҲҶtО»min, as shown in the inset.
As n increases, the asymptotic (П„ вҶ’ вҲһ) generalization loss Lgen decreases, indicating a reduced
overfitting. For n > nвҲ—
(p) = p, although some overfitting remains (i.e., Lgen > 0), the value of
Lgen is sensibly reduced, and the model is no longer expressive enough to memorize the training
data, as shown in [15]. This regime corresponds to the Architectural Regularization phase in Fig. 1.
We show in Fig. 5 (panel C) how the generalization loss Lgen varies in the (n, p) plane depending
on the time П„ at which training is stopped. In agreement with the above results, we find that the
generalizationвҖ“memorization transition line depends on П„ and moves upward for larger values of П„ ,
similarly to the numerical results exposed in Fig. 3 and the illustration in Fig. 1.
4 Conclusions
We have shown that the training dynamics of neural network-based score functions display a form
of implicit regularization that prevents memorization even in highly overparameterized diffusion
models. Specifically, we have identified two well-separated timescales in the learning: П„gen, at
which models begins to generate high-quality, novel samples, and П„mem, beyond which they start to
memorize the training data. The gap between these timescales grows with the size of the training
set, leading to a broad window where early stopped models generate novel samples of high-quality.
We have demonstrated that this phenomenon happens in realistic settings, for controlled synthetic
data, and in analytically tractable models. Although our analysis focuses on DMs, the underlying
score-learning mechanism we uncover is common to all score-based generative models such as
stochastic interpolants [3] or flow matching [32]; we therefore expect our results to generalize to this
broader class.
Limitations and future works.
вҖў While we derived our results under SGD optimization, most DMs are trained in practice with
Adam [28]. In SM Sects. A and D, we show that the two key timescales still arise using Adam,
although with much fewer optimization steps. Studying how different optimizers shift these
timescales would be valuable for practical usage.
вҖў All experiments in Sect. 2 are conducted with unconditional DMs. We additionally verify in
SM Sect. B, using a toy Gaussian mixture dataset and classifier-free guidance [21], that the
same scaling of П„mem with n holds in the conditional settings. Understanding precisely how
the absolute timescales П„mem and П„gen depend on the conditioning remains an open question.
вҖў Our numerical experiments cover a range of p between 1M and 16M. Exploring a wider range
is essential to map the full (n, p) phase diagram sketched in Fig. 1 and understand the precise
effect of expressivity on dynamical regularization.
вҖў Finally, our theoretical analysis rely on well-controlled data and score models that reproduce
the core effects. Extending these analytical frameworks to richer data distributions (such as
Gaussian mixtures or data from the hidden manifold model) and to structured architectures
would be valuable to further characterize the implicit dynamical regularization of training
score-functions. In particular investigating how heavy-tailed data distribution [2] affect the
picture described here could be valuable.
вҖў Although DMs trained on large and diverse datasets likely avoid the memorization regime
we study here, some industrial models were shown to exhibit partial memorization [9, 50].
Our results provide practical guidelines (early-stopping, control the network capacity) to train
DMs robustly and hence avoid memorization, which can be especially helpful in data-scarce
domains (e.g., physical sciences).

Why Diffusion Models DonвҖҷt Memorize:
The Role of Implicit Dynamical Regularization in Training
Supplementary Material (SM)
Tony BonnaireвҖ 
, RaphaГ«l UrfinвҖ 
, Giulio Biroli, Marc MГ©zard
This document provides detailed derivations and additional experiments supporting the main text
(MT). In Sect. A, we give details about the numerical experiments carried out in Sect. 2. In Sect. B
we provide additional numerical experiments on simplified score and data models. Sect. C gives
formal proofs of the main theorems of Sect. 3. Finally, Sect. D exposes more details on the numerical
experiments of Sect. 3.
A Numerical experiments on CelebA
A.1 Details on the numerical setup
Dataset. All numerical experiments in Sect. 2 of the MT use the CelebA face dataset [34]. We
center-crop each RGB image to 32 Г— 32 pixels and convert to grayscale images in order to accelerate
the training of our Diffusion Models (DMs). To precisely control the samples seen by a model,
no data augmentation is applied, and we vary the training set size n in the window [128, 32768].
Examples of training samples are shown in the left-most block of Fig. 6.
Architecture. As commonly done in DDPMs implementations [e.g., 20, 55], the network approximating the score function is a U-Net [46] made of three resolution levels, each containing two
residual blocks with channel multipliers {1, 2, 4} respectively. We apply attention to the two coarsest
resolutions, and embed the diffusion time via sinusoidal position embedding [56]. The base channel
width W varies from 16 to 64 depending on the experiment, resulting in a total of 1 to 16 million
trainable parameters.
Time reparameterization. Compared to the framework presented in the MT, the DDPMs we
train make use of a time reparameterization of the forward and backward processes with a variance
schedule {ОІt

Training. All DMs are trained with Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) at fixed learning rate
О· = 0.01, fixed momentum ОІ = 0.95 and batch size B = min(n, 512). We focus on SGD
to facilitate the analysis of time scaling, avoiding problems that may cause alternative adaptive
optimization schemes like Adam [28]. We train each model for at least 2M SGD steps, sometimes
more for large values of n displaying memorization only later. We do not employ exponential moving
average or learning-rate warm-up.
Generation. To accelerate sampling while preserving FID, we employ the DDIM sampler of Song
et al. (2022) which replaces the Markovian reverse SDE with a deterministic, non-Markovian update.
Given a trained denoiser ОҫОё(xt, t), we iterate for t = T
